policy_statement: Work with neighborhood districts to develop on-street parking management and other access strategies.
mechanism_description: The city works with neighborhood districts to develop on-street parking management and access strategies that reduce driving demand and associated transportation emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Travel/parking policy → ↓ car trips & ↓ VMT (and ↓ cruising) → ↓ fuel use → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city changes parking availability, pricing, time limits, permits, and access/curb management to make solo driving less attractive and to prioritize higher-occupancy modes and non-auto access.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced VMT and reduced idling/cruising decrease gasoline/diesel combustion in on-road vehicles.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower tailpipe CO₂ (and some co-pollutant) emissions from the transportation sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced driving/vehicle miles traveled, leading to lower transportation GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Parking management changes the generalized cost of driving, reducing car trips/VMT (and cruising), which reduces fuel combustion and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although these strategies can improve local air quality, reduce congestion, and sometimes improve land-use efficiency, their main climate significance is direct transport emissions mitigation via reduced driving demand.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a parking strategy primarily increases parking supply or lowers parking cost, it could increase VMT; such cases would not fit this mechanism as stated (“reduce driving demand”). Revenue recycling or mode-shift investments may add resource-efficiency co-benefits but do not replace the dominant mitigation pathway.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism category is defined by using parking/access management to reduce driving demand; therefore the primary category is Mitigation for the whole mechanism class.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy’s main lever is pricing, permits, curb reallocation, or zoning is implementation-specific and should be assigned at the individual policy level with supporting evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge case if “parking management” is implemented as added parking capacity or cheaper parking (which could induce driving); however, the mechanism explicitly targets demand reduction, so classification as Mitigation is appropriate.
key_indicators: "Work with neighborhood districts"; "develop"; "on-street parking management"; "other access strategies"
co_benefits: Reduced congestion/cruising, improved local access and curbside safety, potential air-quality/noise improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: Text does not specify specific instruments (e.g., pricing, permits, time limits), so it is classified as planning-strategy rather than pricing-mechanism or regulatory-mandate.
